summary: Gruber delivers a thorough critique of Walter Isaacson's Steve Jobs biography, arguing that its most fundamental flaw is misunderstanding the centrality of software and design to Jobs's career. He demonstrates that Isaacson treats 'design' as superficial aesthetics rather than Jobs's actual philosophy that 'design is how it works.' Gruber shows how Isaacson uncritically accepts Bill Gates's dismissal of NeXT's software as irrelevant, when in fact NeXTStep became the foundation of Mac OS X and iOS — arguably Jobs's most important legacy. The piece argues that Isaacson's mistrust of Jobs led him to rely on sources like Gates who had obvious conflicts of interest, resulting in a book that documents Jobs the person but profoundly misrepresents Jobs the technologist.
key_points: Isaacson fundamentally misunderstands Jobs's concept of design, treating it as superficial aesthetics rather than the integration of engineering and user experience
The book uncritically presents Bill Gates's factually wrong dismissal of NeXT's technology, never noting that NeXTStep became the foundation of both Mac OS X and iOS
Isaacson's skepticism of Jobs's 'reality distortion field' led him to distrust accurate claims while accepting inaccurate ones from conflicted sources like Gates
The most important arc of Jobs's career — the NeXT software system that saved Apple and powered the iPhone and iPad — is the very thing Isaacson gets wrong
The book succeeds as personal biography but fails as a record of Jobs's actual work and technological contributions
money_quotes: Apple is an experience company. That they create both hardware and software is part of creating the entire product experience.
Post-Jobs, engineering became a component of the design process. This shift made all the difference in the world.
It's almost impossible to overstate just how wrong Bill Gates is here, but Isaacson presents Gates's side as the truth.
It's not just that Isaacson was wrong about something; it's that he was wrong about the most important thing in Jobs's career.

Isaacson gives us the story of an asshole. But the world is full of assholes. What we need is the story of the one man who spearheaded so many remarkable products and who built an amazing and unique company.

Jobs tells Isaacson something that is true, but Isaacson doesn't believe it, and he then quotes someone else, like Bill Gates, saying something that is false to refute it, and Isaacson lets that remark stand as fact.
You could learn more about Steve Jobs's work by reading Rob Walker's 2003 New York Times Magazine piece than by reading Isaacson's book.
key_insight: Isaacson's biography gets the most important thing wrong about Steve Jobs: that his life's work was the software system started at NeXT, which became the foundation of every Apple product that followed.
